chunk_summary: Guidelines for measles vaccination timing and routine immunization catch-up in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), emphasizing vaccination during discharge and follow-up care to prevent high-risk complications like measles fatalities.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of measles vaccination timing in SAM management.
Learn how to operationalize immunization checks during admission, discharge, and follow-up visits.
Recognize the distinction between measles vaccination and vitamin A megadosing protocols.
Develop strategies to link families to routine immunization sessions and address missed appointments.
key_facts: Measles case fatality is higher in malnourished children.
Administer measles-containing vaccine to eligible SAM children ≥6 months, but delay if the child is in shock or has acute instability.
Early measles vaccination before 9 months requires follow-up doses per the national schedule after 9 months.
Immunization status should be reassessed at each weekly outpatient visit.
Vitamin A megadosing is indication-based and distinct from preventive measles vaccination.
reference_quote: In standard hospital guidance, give a measles-containing vaccine to eligible children with SAM who are ≥6 months and are unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated, but delay vaccination if the child is in shock or has uncontrolled acute instability. (WHO Pocket Book of Hospital Care for Children)
document_key_topics: Discharge and follow-up care for malnourished children
Vitamin A protocols in SAM management
